saida_bruta:
No trace of the Colossus marked the appearance of the middle-aged man stirring to consciousness on the screened veranda of a gambrel-roofed house in the prosperous Zenith neighborhood of Floral Heights.

This was George F. Babbitt. Approaching forty-six in this spring of 1920, he produced no tangible goods, neither footwear nor dairy nor verses, yet he excelled at the art of marketing real estate for larger sums than buyers should reasonably spend.

A stout, rosy dome topped his thinning, brittle brown locks. In sleep his features retained an infant-like quality, notwithstanding his creases and the deep crimson grooves left by eyeglasses along his bridge. Though not obese, he showed signs of indulgent eating; his cheeks swelled softly, and the smooth hand resting inertly on the olive-drab coverlet looked somewhat swollen. He exuded financial security, domestic stability, and a lack of passion; equally devoid of poetry was this porch, which faced a sturdy elm tree, two neat patches of lawn, a paved driveway, and a metal garage. Nevertheless, Babbitt remained enthralled by his fantasy of the fairy girl, a vision far more exotic than ruby temples near a moonlit ocean.

He exuded financial security, domestic stability, and a lack of passion; equally devoid of poetry was this porch, which faced a sturdy elm tree, two neat patches of lawn, a paved driveway, and a metal garage. Nevertheless, Babbitt remained enthralled by his fantasy of the fairy girl, a vision far more exotic than ruby temples near a moonlit ocean.

saida_bruta:
Nothing monumental suggested the presence of the middle-aged occupant waking up on the enclosed balcony of a Dutch-colonial dwelling in the affluent Zenith suburb of Floral Heights.

His name was George F. Babbitt. Nearing forty-six in the spring season of 1920, he manufactured no material merchandise, neither shoes nor milk nor poetry, but he specialized in the trade of selling property for higher amounts than purchasers ought to pay.

A fleshy, pink forehead crowned his sparse, fragile brown hair. While sleeping his face kept a babyish expression, despite his wrinkles and the dark red marks pressed by spectacles on his nose. Although not fat, he betrayed a love for food; his jowls bulged slightly, and the pale arm lying still on the khaki-colored blanket appeared rather puffy. He embodied economic comfort, family reliability, and a clean absence of desire; just as unpoetic was this veranda, which overlooked a solid elm, twin tidy plots of grass, a concrete path, and a steel garage. Yet, Babbitt stayed captivated by his dream of the fairy maiden, an image much more enchanting than ivory towers beside a glowing sea.

saida_bruta:
No grand markers indicated that a middle-aged resident was stirring on the screened-in porch of a Dutch-colonial house situated within the wealthy Zenith neighborhood of Floral Heights.

This man was George F. Babbitt. Approaching forty-six during the spring of 1920, he did not produce physical goods, like shoes, milk, or poetry; instead, he made his living by selling real estate for more than the buyers should have spent.

A plump, rosy brow sat beneath his thin, delicate brown hair. In slumber, his features maintained an infant-like look, notwithstanding his creases and the deep red indentations left by glasses on his nose. Not truly obese, he nevertheless showed a fondness for eating; his cheeks sagged a bit, and the pale arm resting quietly on the tan blanket seemed somewhat soft. He represented financial stability, domestic responsibility, and an absolute lack of passion; equally unromantic was this balcony, which faced an established elm, two neat lawns, a cement walkway, and a metal garage. Still, Babbitt remained spellbound by his fantasy of the fairy child, a vision far more magical than ivory towers overlooking a shimmering sea.

His thin, soft brown hair rested above a plump, rosy forehead. Deep sleep maintained a youthfully simple look on his face, notwithstanding his creases and the deep red indentations pressed into his nose by spectacles. Not particularly large, he nevertheless revealed a passion for dining; his cheeks sagged a bit, and the pale arm resting still upon the cream-colored blanket looked exceptionally soft. He personified financial prosperity, domestic obligation, and a complete absence of passion; of equal mediocrity was this porch, which faced a mature elm, two manicured lawns, a cement walkway, and a metal garage. Still, Babbitt was enchanted by his vision of the legendary woodland fairy, a being far more beautiful than marble mansions towering over a brilliant sea.
